title: Spindle Cell Tumor Workup (Soft Tissue)
summary: A minimal, type-valid pathway: start → morphology gate → decision → result.

n1.title: Start
n1.description: Review clinical context (age, site, depth), imaging, and gross. Confirm this is a spindle cell predominant lesion on H&E.

options.label: Proceed to morphology gate

n2.title: Morphology gate
n2.description: Confirm key pattern-level features before ordering broad panels.
morphologyFeatures: Growth pattern: fascicular / storiform / patternless / herringbone
Vascular pattern: staghorn / branching / delicate capillaries

options.label: Patternless + staghorn vessels (consider SFT)
options.rationale: Classic architectural clue for SFT spectrum.

options.label: Fascicular + eosinophilic cytoplasm (consider SMC)
options.rationale: Smooth muscle morphology suggests SMA/Desmin.

options.label: Wavy nuclei / nerve association (consider PNST)
options.rationale: PNST clues push S100/SOX10.

n3.title: Decision: SFT pathway
n3.description: If SFT is suspected, confirm with lineage-defining markers and exclude mimics.
molecularIndications: NAB2-STAT6 fusion testing if STAT6 equivocal or for challenging cases

options.label: Confirm SFT
Not SFT / equivocal

n4.title: Decision: Smooth muscle pathway
n4.description: Confirm smooth muscle differentiation and assess malignancy parameters.

options.label: Confirm smooth muscle tumor
Equivocal smooth muscle

n5.title: Decision: PNST pathway
n5.description: Confirm neural crest differentiation and consider site-specific entities.

options.label: Confirm PNST
Not PNST / equivocal

n6.title: Result: Solitary fibrous tumor favored
n6.description: Integrate morphology + STAT6 nuclear positivity. Risk stratify by size, mitoses, necrosis, and age as applicable.
confirmatoryStudies: STAT6 (nuclear)
Consider fusion testing if STAT6 negative/equivocal
pearls: Patternless architecture + staghorn vasculature is a strong clue.
STAT6 is the key confirmatory marker.
pitfalls: CD34 is supportive but not specific.
Beware cellular SFT vs other hypercellular spindle tumors.

n7.title: Stop: broaden differential
n7.description: If lineage is unclear, stop and broaden workup (consider keratin-positive sarcomatoid carcinoma, melanoma, synovial sarcoma, MPNST, etc.). Use targeted panels and correlate with imaging/gross.
stopConditions: Unexpected keratin positivity
Strong SOX10 with atypical morphology
Discrepant clinical/radiologic scenario

n8.title: Result: Smooth muscle tumor favored
n8.description: If SMA/Desmin/H-caldesmon support SMC differentiation, classify based on site and malignant criteria.
confirmatoryStudies: SMA
Desmin
H-caldesmon
pearls: Fascicles + blunt-ended nuclei + eosinophilic cytoplasm support SMC.
pitfalls: Myofibroblastic lesions can be SMA+ but desmin/H-caldesmon-.

n9.title: Result: PNST favored
n9.description: If S100/SOX10 support neural differentiation, subclassify with clinicopathologic correlation and consider perineurial markers as appropriate.
confirmatoryStudies: SOX10
EMA/Claudin-1/GLUT1 if perineurial differentiation suspected
pearls: SOX10 is often more specific for Schwannian differentiation than S100 alone.
pitfalls: Melanocytic lesions can mimic PNST and share SOX10 positivity.

title: Lytic Bone Lesion: Giant Cell–Rich Differential
summary: Minimal pathway for giant cell–rich lytic bone lesions: start → decision → result/stop.

b1.title: Start
b1.description: Confirm radiology: lytic vs sclerotic, epiphyseal vs metaphyseal, and patient age. Review H&E for giant cell–rich pattern.

options.label: Proceed to decision node

b2.title: Decision: giant cell–rich patterns
b2.description: Use radiology + distribution of osteoclast-type giant cells and stromal cytology.

options.label: Epiphyseal lesion in skeletally mature, uniform giant cells
Aneurysmal spaces / blood-filled cystic change
High-grade atypia / malignant osteoid

b3.title: Result: Giant cell tumor of bone favored
b3.description: Correlate with epiphyseal location; consider H3F3A (G34) mutation testing if needed.
confirmatoryStudies: H3F3A mutation testing (as needed)
pearls: Mononuclear stromal cells are the neoplastic population.
pitfalls: Beware giant cell–rich osteosarcoma if malignant osteoid present.

b4.title: Result: Aneurysmal bone cyst pattern
b4.description: Primary ABC can show USP6 rearrangement; secondary ABC occurs over other lesions.
confirmatoryStudies: USP6 rearrangement testing if primary ABC suspected
pearls: Always exclude underlying lesion in secondary ABC.
pitfalls: Telangiectatic osteosarcoma can mimic ABC radiologically and grossly.

b5.title: Stop: consider aggressive/malignant bone tumor
b5.description: If malignant osteoid or marked atypia is present, stop and evaluate for osteosarcoma subtype (including telangiectatic) with full radiologic correlation and sampling.
stopConditions: Malignant lace-like osteoid
Marked nuclear pleomorphism in stromal cells
Atypical mitoses

title: QC Failure Response
summary: Operational triage for a QC failure: characterize the control pattern, protect patient reporting, and choose the next corrective action.

q1.title: Start
q1.description: Pause auto-release for the affected assay or bench, confirm which control level failed, and review the most recent Levey-Jennings pattern before touching patient samples.

options.label: Characterize the QC signal

q2.title: Decision: characterize the QC signal
q2.description: Separate isolated random-error patterns from systematic failure and post-change instability.

options.label: Single control point outside limits without a broader pattern
options.rationale: A lone miss may reflect random error, but patient reporting still stays on hold until you verify the run.

options.label: Shift, trend, or multirule violation across levels or runs
options.rationale: This pattern is more consistent with systematic error and needs broader containment.

options.label: Failure appeared after calibration, reagent lot change, or maintenance
options.rationale: Post-change instability often needs verification logic before routine testing resumes.

q3.title: Result: random-error check with reporting hold
q3.description: Repeat the affected control level, inspect preparation and instrument handling, and resume patient testing only after the rerun is acceptable.
confirmatoryStudies: Repeat control material at the failed level
Review recent Levey-Jennings position for the same level
pearls: A single isolated miss is not a free pass to release patient results.
Random-error thinking still starts with containment.
pitfalls: Do not ignore a failed control because one other level remained in range.
Do not rerun patient samples before the control problem is resolved.
q3.relatedTutorialQuery: Management and Informatics: Levey-Jennings QC Studio

q4.title: Result: systematic-error response
q4.description: Treat the event as a probable method or instrument problem, broaden the hold, and use rule-based troubleshooting before restarting the assay.
confirmatoryStudies: Review Westgard rule pattern across all affected control levels
Inspect calibration status, reagent integrity, and recent maintenance events
pearls: Shift and trend patterns matter more than any single point.
A multirule event is an assay-process problem until proven otherwise.
pitfalls: Do not correct a systematic problem by repeated blind reruns.
Do not restart patient reporting without documenting the corrective action.
q4.relatedTutorialQuery: Management and Informatics: Westgard Rules Studio

q5.title: Decision: post-change instability
q5.description: If the failure followed a method change event, decide whether the problem is already patient-facing or still confined to verification work.

options.label: Patient results may already be affected or the assay is safety-critical
options.rationale: Escalation and communication come before routine restart.

options.label: No patient reporting yet; the issue is contained within startup verification
options.rationale: You can stay in verification mode while proving the assay is stable.

q6.title: Stop: protect patients and escalate
q6.description: Hold affected reporting, notify supervisory leadership, assess whether corrected reports or clinician communication are needed, and document the event as a patient-safety issue.
stopConditions: Critical-value workflow may be affected
Previously released patient results may be unreliable
The assay cannot be stabilized promptly
q6.relatedTutorialQuery: Management and Informatics: Major Sources of Laboratory Errors Studio

q7.title: Result: complete verification before restart
q7.description: Finish the defined verification plan, confirm acceptable control performance after the corrective action, and document restart criteria before patient testing resumes.
confirmatoryStudies: Verification checks appropriate to the post-change event
Documented restart criteria after corrective action
pearls: Validation and verification are operational decisions, not interchangeable labels.
A stable control recovery needs documented acceptance criteria.
pitfalls: Do not treat a post-change QC failure as routine noise.
Do not relabel validation work as verification just to shorten the restart path.
q7.relatedTutorialQuery: Management and Informatics: Validation vs Verification Studio

title: Validation-Versus-Verification Triage
summary: Choose the right oversight path when a lab is bringing on a test, modifying an assay, or restarting a changed method.

v1.title: Start
v1.description: Define what changed before choosing the oversight frame: a new test claim, a modified method, or a local implementation of an already established method.

options.label: Classify the implementation scenario

v2.title: Decision: what kind of change is this?
v2.description: Separate truly new or materially altered claims from local confirmation of expected performance.

options.label: The lab is implementing an established method without materially changing the claim
options.rationale: This usually favors local verification rather than full validation.

options.label: The method, specimen use, reportable range, or intended use has materially changed
options.rationale: A material claim change pushes the oversight path toward validation.

options.label: The scope of change is still unclear or poorly documented
options.rationale: You need to stop and define the claim before pretending the oversight path is obvious.

v3.title: Result: verification pathway
v3.description: Use a local verification plan that confirms the method performs as expected in your setting before go-live.
confirmatoryStudies: Precision, accuracy, and reportable performance checks appropriate to the local implementation
Documented acceptance criteria before release
pearls: Verification confirms expected performance in a local environment.
The claim is not being reinvented; it is being proven locally.
pitfalls: Do not treat verification as optional because the method is familiar.
Do not skip documentation just because the assay is already in common use elsewhere.
v3.relatedTutorialQuery: Management and Informatics: Validation vs Verification Studio

v4.title: Result: validation pathway
v4.description: Use a validation plan when the lab is establishing or materially altering the test claim, then define the studies needed before clinical release.
confirmatoryStudies: Validation studies matched to the changed claim
Risk-aware review of analytical performance before patient reporting
pearls: A changed claim means the burden of proof is larger than local verification.
Validation is about defending a new or altered use, not just generating paperwork.
pitfalls: Do not relabel material method changes as verification to save time.
Do not go live before the altered claim has explicit supporting evidence.
v4.relatedTutorialQuery: Management and Informatics: Validation vs Verification Studio

v5.title: Stop: define the claim before proceeding
v5.description: Pause the rollout, clarify intended use and method changes, and document the scope with laboratory leadership before choosing verification or validation.
stopConditions: Intended use is ambiguous
Method changes are not fully documented
Leadership approval or oversight scope is unresolved
v5.relatedTutorialQuery: Management and Informatics: Major Sources of Laboratory Errors Studio

title: LIS Workflow Redesign
summary: Redesign an unsafe reporting workflow by deciding whether interfacing, middleware, or process redesign is the highest-yield safety intervention.

l1.title: Start
l1.description: Map the current result path from instrument or source system to the final report, and identify where repeated manual handling occurs.

options.label: Classify the workflow vulnerability

l2.title: Decision: what is the main workflow failure mode?
l2.description: Choose the intervention by matching it to the dominant risk rather than defaulting to a favorite informatics tool.

options.label: Repeated manual transcription or copy-forward between systems
options.rationale: Structured interfacing is strongest when the recurring risk is transcription or data-transfer error.

options.label: Rule-based routing, reflexing, or queue management is inconsistent across cases
options.rationale: Middleware is useful when the safety gain depends on standardized decision logic between systems.

options.label: The failure is mostly handoff design, staffing, or local process confusion
options.rationale: Not every unsafe workflow is solved by another software layer.

l3.title: Result: interface the workflow
l3.description: Use LIS or source-system interfacing to remove repeated manual entry and preserve structured result transmission.
confirmatoryStudies: Map source-to-destination fields and exception handling
Verify that critical result and corrected report paths survive the interface
pearls: An interface is a patient-safety intervention when it removes recurring transcription risk.
Structured connectivity is strongest when the same manual step is repeated at scale.
pitfalls: Do not treat manual entry as operationally equivalent to interfacing.
Do not ignore exception-path behavior while celebrating the primary happy path.
l3.relatedTutorialQuery: Management and Informatics: LIS Interfacing Studio

l4.title: Result: standardize with middleware or rules logic
l4.description: Use middleware or governed rules logic when the main hazard is inconsistent routing, reflex behavior, or queue prioritization across repeated cases.
confirmatoryStudies: Documented routing and reflex rules
Validation or verification of rule behavior before clinical use
pearls: Middleware helps when the risk lives in inconsistent operational decisions between systems.
Standardized rules are safer than ad hoc human memory for repetitive pathways.
pitfalls: Do not add rules logic without proving the edge cases.
Do not assume middleware fixes a process that was never mapped clearly.
l4.relatedTutorialQuery: Management and Informatics: Validation vs Verification Studio

l5.title: Result: redesign the local process first
l5.description: Redesign the handoff, staffing, or communication workflow when the main failure is local process design rather than missing software connectivity.
confirmatoryStudies: Defined ownership for each handoff step
A documented safer-state workflow before reintroducing automation
pearls: A bad process wrapped in software is still a bad process.
Workflow clarity is often the first safety intervention.
pitfalls: Do not buy informatics complexity to avoid naming the real process flaw.
Do not leave corrected-report or critical-value ownership ambiguous.
l5.relatedTutorialQuery: Management and Informatics: Major Sources of Laboratory Errors Studio

title: Finance-Aware Assay Planning
summary: Plan an assay rollout by balancing break-even logic, acquisition structure, and operational burden before bringing testing in-house.

f1.title: Start
f1.description: Frame the proposed assay around forecasted volume, contribution margin, acquisition structure, and who will absorb the new operational workload.

options.label: Choose the dominant planning constraint

f2.title: Decision: what is the main planning constraint?
f2.description: Pick the operational bottleneck before committing to in-house rollout or a platform contract.

options.label: Projected volume remains below break-even for internalization
options.rationale: A weak volume forecast is often the clearest reason to delay internal rollout.

options.label: Volume is plausible, but the acquisition model may hide downstream cost or lock-in
options.rationale: Reagent-rental and related structures can look attractive until the contract terms are unpacked.

options.label: The apparent financial win may collapse under staffing, turnaround, or quality burden
options.rationale: An assay can be mathematically viable and still operationally unsound.

f3.title: Result: keep as send-out or defer rollout
f3.description: If the projected volume remains well below break-even, the safer immediate decision is often to continue send-out testing or delay launch until utilization changes.
confirmatoryStudies: Break-even calculation using fixed cost and contribution margin
Volume forecast tied to realistic test demand

workedExamples.title: Break-even volume
steps: Set reimbursement at $55 per test and variable cost at $15 per test.
Calculate break-even volume: $120,000 / $40 = 3,000 tests per year.
If projected demand is 1,800 tests per year, the assay remains below break-even.
workedExamples.takeaway: A strong reimbursement number does not justify internal rollout when realistic demand remains well below the break-even volume.

pearls: A financially attractive reimbursement rate does not rescue low-volume internalization.
Break-even is an operational decision tool, not just a formula exercise.
pitfalls: Do not internalize testing just because the reimbursement looks strong.
Do not ignore fixed cost recovery when judging apparent profitability.
f3.relatedTutorialQuery: Management and Informatics: Break-Even Analysis Studio

f4.title: Result: interrogate the acquisition structure
f4.description: Compare reagent-rental and direct-purchase logic using total per-reportable-test cost, service obligations, and contract drag before choosing a platform path.
confirmatoryStudies: Total cost comparison across acquisition models
Review of service, minimum-volume, and reagent-commitment terms

workedExamples.title: Reagent-rental versus direct purchase
steps: Estimate direct purchase annualized instrument cost at $40,000 and variable reagent cost at $18 per reportable test.
Estimate reagent-rental instrument cost at $0 upfront but variable reagent cost at $28 per reportable test plus a 2,500-test minimum.
At 3,000 tests per year, direct purchase total cost is $40,000 + (3,000 x $18) = $94,000.
At 3,000 tests per year, reagent rental total cost is 3,000 x $28 = $84,000 before service and contract penalties.
Recheck the comparison once service fees, minimum-volume penalties, and reagent commitment clauses are added.
workedExamples.takeaway: The cheaper-looking platform on first pass may stop being favorable once contract drag and real utilization are included.

pearls: Reagent rental can reduce upfront capital pressure while increasing downstream operational constraint.
The acquisition model matters because it changes the real cost of each reportable result.
pitfalls: Do not assume reagent rental is automatically the low-risk option.
Do not compare platforms without looking at the contract terms that shape real utilization.
f4.relatedTutorialQuery: Management and Informatics: Reagent Rental Decision Studio

f5.title: Result: stop for operational capacity review
f5.description: Reassess staffing, turnaround expectations, and quality burden before rollout when the hidden constraint is workforce or process capacity rather than purchase price alone.
confirmatoryStudies: Staffing and throughput review for the affected section
Turnaround-time and quality-risk assessment for the proposed workflow
pearls: A mathematically favorable assay can still fail if the section cannot absorb it safely.
Productivity metrics need quality and turnaround context to be meaningful.
pitfalls: Do not use raw volume as the only proxy for readiness.
Do not call a rollout efficient if it depends on fragile staffing workarounds.
f5.relatedTutorialQuery: Management and Informatics: Staff Productivity Studio

title: Anemia Workup and Hemolysis Triage
summary: Use CBC indices, reticulocyte response, and hemolysis cues to decide whether anemia reflects blood loss or hemolysis, reduced production, or urgent marrow pathology.

a1.title: Start
a1.description: Begin with hemoglobin, MCV, reticulocyte response, smear clues, and whether other blood cell lines are also abnormal.

options.label: Classify the anemia frame

a2.title: Decision: what is the first diagnostic frame?
a2.description: Separate increased red-cell loss from impaired production and from patterns that need urgent marrow-level escalation.

options.label: Reticulocytes are appropriately elevated or hemolysis or blood-loss clues are present
options.rationale: A brisk marrow response pushes the differential toward red-cell loss rather than failure to produce.

options.label: Reticulocytes are low or inappropriately normal, suggesting a production problem
options.rationale: Poor marrow response reframes the workup around iron, nutrient, kidney, endocrine, inflammatory, or marrow causes.

options.label: Additional cytopenias, circulating blasts, or marked smear dysplasia raise concern for marrow pathology
options.rationale: This pattern should not be flattened into routine outpatient anemia workup.

a3.title: Result: hemolysis or blood-loss pathway
a3.description: Confirm whether the elevated reticulocyte response reflects hemolysis or active blood loss, then choose targeted follow-up studies rather than broad screening.
confirmatoryStudies: Reticulocyte count with smear review
LDH, indirect bilirubin, and haptoglobin
DAT when immune hemolysis is plausible
pearls: Reticulocytosis is a physiologic clue, not the final diagnosis.
DAT belongs in the hemolysis branch, not the default anemia screen.
pitfalls: Do not call anemia hypoproliferative when the reticulocyte response is clearly brisk.
Do not miss immune hemolysis when the smear and chemistry pattern already support red-cell destruction.
a3.relatedTutorialQuery: DAT and Hemolytic Transfusion Reaction Studio

a4.title: Decision: what kind of production problem is most likely?
a4.description: Use indices and context to distinguish iron-restricted states from macrocytic or marrow-linked patterns.

options.label: Microcytosis or iron-restricted physiology is most likely
options.rationale: Iron deficiency, inflammation, and related states stay near the top when the indices point that way.

options.label: Macrocytosis, multilineage abnormalities, or renal or endocrine context changes the frame
options.rationale: Macrocytic and broader cytopenic patterns often need nutrient, systemic, or marrow-level reasoning.

a5.title: Stop: urgent marrow or hematopathology escalation
a5.description: Pause routine anemia sorting and escalate when blasts, pancytopenia, or strong dysplasia suggest a marrow-centered process that needs same-day hematopathology reasoning.
stopConditions: Blasts or highly suspicious immature forms on smear
Pancytopenia or multilineage failure pattern
Marked dysplasia or other concern for acute leukemia or marrow infiltration

a6.title: Result: iron-restricted or microcytic production pathway
a6.description: Use iron studies and clinical context to decide whether the anemia reflects true iron deficiency, inflammatory restriction, or a less common microcytic disorder.
confirmatoryStudies: Ferritin and transferrin saturation or iron studies
Peripheral smear review for microcytic morphology
Targeted hemoglobinopathy testing when history or indices support it
pearls: Low MCV narrows the frame, but ferritin and context still matter.
Microcytosis is a routing clue, not proof of one single diagnosis.
pitfalls: Do not collapse all microcytic anemia into iron deficiency without iron studies.
Do not forget that inflammation can mimic iron deficiency in the lab pattern.

a7.title: Result: macrocytic or marrow-linked production pathway
a7.description: Check nutrient, renal, endocrine, medication, and marrow-linked causes when the anemia pattern is macrocytic or broader than isolated iron restriction.
confirmatoryStudies: B12 and folate assessment when clinically appropriate
Renal or endocrine context review with chemistry correlation
Marrow-directed evaluation if cytopenias or dysplasia persist
pearls: Macrocytosis broadens the workup beyond simple iron logic.
Production failure that involves more than one cell line deserves a marrow-level frame.
pitfalls: Do not stop at MCV without looking at the rest of the CBC and smear.
Do not defer marrow escalation when persistent cytopenias and dysplasia are already visible.

title: Bleeding Diathesis and Coagulation Test Interpretation
summary: Use bleeding pattern, platelet data, and coagulation screening tests to decide whether the problem is primary hemostasis, factor pathway dysfunction, or unstable systemic coagulopathy.

b1.title: Start
b1.description: Anchor the workup in the bleeding pattern, medication history, platelet count, and initial PT, aPTT, fibrinogen, and smear context.

options.label: Classify the hemostatic problem

b2.title: Decision: where does the pattern point first?
b2.description: Distinguish platelet or von Willebrand-type bleeding from factor pathway problems and unstable systemic coagulopathy.

options.label: Mucocutaneous bleeding, platelet abnormalities, or von Willebrand-type pattern
options.rationale: Primary hemostasis problems usually declare themselves through the bleeding pattern before factor studies solve the case.

options.label: PT or aPTT abnormalities suggest a factor pathway or inhibitor problem
options.rationale: Screening tests now become the main routing tool.

options.label: Active or unstable bleeding with broad coagulopathy concern
options.rationale: When the patient is unstable, triage and support move faster than elegant diagnostic sequencing.

b3.title: Result: primary hemostasis pathway
b3.description: Prioritize platelet count and smear review, medication exposure, and von Willebrand testing when the pattern fits primary hemostasis rather than coagulation-factor deficiency.
confirmatoryStudies: Platelet count with peripheral smear review
von Willebrand antigen and activity testing when indicated
Medication and qualitative platelet dysfunction review
pearls: The bleeding pattern matters as much as the screening numbers.
Normal PT and aPTT do not exclude clinically important platelet or von Willebrand disorders.
pitfalls: Do not order factor cascades first when the history already points to primary hemostasis.
Do not ignore medication-related platelet dysfunction.

b4.title: Decision: what do the screening tests imply?
b4.description: Use the PT and aPTT pattern to separate intrinsic-pathway issues from broader consumptive or multifactor abnormalities.

options.label: Isolated aPTT abnormality or targeted factor or inhibitor question
options.rationale: A focused intrinsic-pathway problem often needs mixing-study logic and factor-directed follow-up.

options.label: Multiple screening tests are abnormal or fibrinogen is low
options.rationale: Combined abnormalities broaden the frame toward systemic coagulopathy.

b5.title: Stop: unstable bleeding or systemic coagulopathy response
b5.description: Escalate immediately when the patient has unstable bleeding, suspected DIC, severe liver-related coagulopathy, or a situation where supportive transfusion decisions cannot wait.
stopConditions: Hemodynamic instability or major active bleeding
Possible DIC or severe multifactor coagulopathy
Immediate component-support decisions are required

b6.title: Result: factor deficiency or inhibitor pathway
b6.description: Use mixing-study logic and targeted factor evaluation when the main clue is an isolated aPTT or a narrower factor-pathway problem.
confirmatoryStudies: Mixing study when appropriate
Targeted factor assays or inhibitor evaluation
Clinical review for anticoagulant exposure
pearls: An isolated screening-test abnormality should narrow the follow-up rather than trigger indiscriminate testing.
Mixing studies answer a routing question: deficiency versus inhibitor.
pitfalls: Do not interpret an abnormal aPTT without considering anticoagulant effect.
Do not skip targeted follow-up once the screening pattern is already focused.

b7.title: Result: consumptive or multifactor coagulopathy pathway
b7.description: Broaden the frame to DIC, liver-related dysfunction, dilutional states, or other multifactor causes when several screening studies move together.
confirmatoryStudies: Fibrinogen and broader coagulation-panel correlation
Clinical review for DIC, liver dysfunction, massive transfusion, or anticoagulant exposure
Supportive transfusion planning when clinically indicated
pearls: Multiple abnormal screening tests usually mean the problem is larger than a single factor deficiency.
The clinical context decides whether the next step is diagnosis, support, or both.
pitfalls: Do not force a single-factor explanation onto a global coagulopathy pattern.
Do not separate coagulation interpretation from the severity of active bleeding.
b7.relatedTutorialQuery: DAT and Hemolytic Transfusion Reaction Studio

title: Specimen-to-Result CP Workflow
summary: Move a Clinical Pathology request from specimen quality triage and specimen integrity through bench selection, result interpretation, and safe reporting without skipping preanalytic or postanalytic safeguards.

s1.title: Start
s1.description: Confirm patient identity, specimen integrity, and the clinical question before choosing a bench pathway or releasing any result.

options.label: Check whether the specimen is fit for use

s2.title: Decision: is the specimen usable for the requested question?
s2.description: A flawed specimen should redirect the workflow before deeper bench reasoning begins.

options.label: Misidentification, contamination, or collection-quality problems make the sample unsafe
options.rationale: Preanalytic failure is still the highest-yield finding when the specimen itself is compromised.

options.label: The specimen is acceptable, but the next step depends on the testing lane
options.rationale: Once integrity is acceptable, the question becomes which CP branch should own the workup.

s3.title: Stop: recollect or correct before testing
s3.description: Do not push a bad specimen through the lab. Resolve identity or collection issues, communicate the problem, and recollect when needed.
stopConditions: Identity mismatch or unlabeled specimen
Collection or contamination problem invalidates interpretation
The specimen quality makes downstream testing misleading
s3.relatedTutorialQuery: Management and Informatics: Major Sources of Laboratory Errors Studio

s4.title: Decision: which CP lane owns the next action?
s4.description: Route the specimen into the branch where the main interpretive or safety question actually lives.

options.label: Culture setup, organism workup, or susceptibility logic is central
options.rationale: Microbiology workflow depends on specimen quality, media strategy, and AST consequences.

options.label: Chemistry or endocrine interpretation and critical-result management are central
options.rationale: Chemistry workups often pivot on analyte interpretation and method-aware critical reporting.

options.label: Identity-sensitive blood bank or hemolysis-related reasoning is central
options.rationale: Transfusion and DAT-linked problems should stay inside their safety-sensitive workflow.

options.label: The weak point is the interface, handoff, or reporting chain itself
options.rationale: Sometimes the specimen is fine and the danger lives in the workflow.

s5.title: Result: microbiology bench workflow
s5.description: Proceed through specimen-quality review, organism workup, and AST interpretation when microbiology owns the main diagnostic and reporting task.
confirmatoryStudies: Specimen-quality assessment before deep interpretation
Bench identification pathway matched to organism class
AST interpretation tied to stewardship and reporting consequences
pearls: Microbiology workflow begins with specimen quality, not just the organism result.
AST categories matter because they change downstream treatment and communication.
pitfalls: Do not over-interpret a poor specimen.
Do not separate AST interpretation from organism identity and reporting consequence.
s5.relatedTutorialQuery: Clinical Microbiology Methods and AST Studio

s6.title: Result: chemistry interpretation and critical-result path
s6.description: Use chemistry or endocrine interpretation logic when the central problem is analyte meaning, method consequence, or critical-value management.
confirmatoryStudies: Context-aware analyte interpretation
Method or specimen considerations that change result trust
Critical-result escalation when clinically required
pearls: A chemistry result is only useful when you trust both the specimen and the method context.
Critical-result communication is part of the workflow, not an afterthought.
pitfalls: Do not treat chemistry interpretation as detached from specimen or method quality.
Do not release a critical result without the appropriate communication pathway.
s6.relatedTutorialQuery: Chemical Pathology Endocrine and Metabolic Studio

s7.title: Result: transfusion or hemolysis-sensitive pathway
s7.description: Keep identity-sensitive compatibility and hemolysis workups inside blood bank or DAT-guided reasoning when the risk of wrong-patient or reaction harm is central.
confirmatoryStudies: Identity and compatibility review
DAT-linked hemolysis workup when clinically indicated
Transfusion-safety communication for urgent events
pearls: Blood bank workflow is safety-first because the specimen identity burden is unusually high.
Hemolysis questions change rapidly when transfusion exposure is part of the history.
pitfalls: Do not flatten blood bank problems into generic chemistry or hematology workflows.
Do not ignore identity-sensitive safeguards when compatibility questions are in play.
s7.relatedTutorialQuery: DAT and Hemolytic Transfusion Reaction Studio

s8.title: Result: workflow or reporting redesign pathway
s8.description: Address interface, middleware, or handoff weakness when the specimen and bench work are sound but the reporting chain is still unsafe.
confirmatoryStudies: Map the manual handoff or interface vulnerability
Decide whether redesign, interfacing, or middleware is the highest-yield fix
Document the reporting risk being reduced
pearls: A technically correct result can still become unsafe in the reporting chain.
Workflow repair belongs in the CP reasoning surface because it changes patient safety.
pitfalls: Do not treat postanalytic risk as less important than bench accuracy.
Do not patch a systemic reporting weakness with repeated manual workarounds.
s8.relatedTutorialQuery: Management and Informatics: LIS Interfacing Studio

title: Transfusion Reaction Triage
summary: Use immediate symptom pattern, transfusion-safety steps, and blood-bank reasoning to separate hemolytic, febrile or allergic, and pulmonary or volume-overload transfusion reactions.

tr1.title: Start
tr1.description: Begin when new symptoms arise during or shortly after transfusion and frame the event first as a transfusion-safety problem rather than a generic anemia or chemistry question.

options.label: Classify the reaction pattern and immediate safety risk

tr2.title: Decision: is this an active transfusion-safety event?
tr2.description: Treat fever, chills, dyspnea, hypotension, flank pain, dark urine, urticaria, or rapid cardiopulmonary change during or after transfusion as a true reaction pathway until proven otherwise.

options.label: Symptoms developed during or soon after transfusion and could reflect reaction harm
options.rationale: Wrong-patient exposure, hemolysis, pulmonary injury, and severe hypersensitivity must be contained before deeper classification.

options.label: The pattern is not convincingly linked to transfusion timing or safety risk
options.rationale: If the event does not behave like a transfusion reaction, do not force it into blood-bank triage.

tr3.title: Stop: stop transfusion and escalate immediately
tr3.description: Stop the transfusion, maintain IV access, notify the blood bank and clinical team, and begin clerical and specimen-identity review before pursuing detailed classification.
stopConditions: Possible hemolysis, hypotension, or respiratory compromise
Identity-sensitive blood-bank harm cannot be excluded
A reaction workup must begin before routine product continuation

tr4.title: Decision: what is the dominant reaction pattern?
tr4.description: After the transfusion is stopped and the event is escalated, separate hemolytic patterns from febrile or allergic reactions and from respiratory or volume-overload hazards.

options.label: Fever, chills, flank pain, dark urine, hemolysis labs, or compatibility-error clues dominate
options.rationale: This pattern needs immediate blood-bank and DAT-linked reasoning.

options.label: Urticaria, pruritus, fever, or anaphylactoid features dominate without clear hemolysis
options.rationale: This favors febrile nonhemolytic, allergic, or anaphylactic reaction pathways.

options.label: Dyspnea, hypoxemia, pulmonary edema, or circulatory-overload features dominate
options.rationale: TRALI and TACO have immediate patient-safety consequences that outrun serologic detail.

tr5.title: Result: hemolytic transfusion reaction workup
tr5.description: Proceed through clerical check, repeat blood-bank compatibility review, hemolysis assessment, and DAT or eluate logic when immune hemolysis is plausible.
confirmatoryStudies: Clerical and patient-unit identity check
Repeat ABO/Rh or compatibility review as indicated
Hemolysis assessment with DAT-linked workup when clinically indicated
pearls: DAT is a workup tool inside a broader transfusion-reaction pathway, not the whole diagnosis.
Identity review belongs early because wrong-patient harm changes everything.
pitfalls: Do not continue classification while the transfusion is still running.
Do not treat a positive DAT as a complete answer without hemolysis and compatibility context.
tr5.relatedTutorialQuery: DAT and Hemolytic Transfusion Reaction Studio

tr6.title: Result: febrile, allergic, or anaphylactic reaction pathway
tr6.description: Reframe the event around febrile nonhemolytic, allergic, or anaphylactic reaction logic and choose the next safest blood-bank and clinical response.
confirmatoryStudies: Reaction-pattern review without overcalling hemolysis
Clinical correlation for allergic versus febrile versus anaphylactic features
Blood-bank documentation and product-safety follow-up
pearls: Not every transfusion reaction is hemolytic, but every real reaction still needs structured safety handling.
The main task is safe classification plus the right immediate response.
pitfalls: Do not default every fever to acute hemolysis.
Do not dismiss severe allergic or anaphylactic patterns as minor reactions.

tr7.title: Result: pulmonary or circulatory hazard pathway
tr7.description: Differentiate TRALI from TACO when respiratory decompensation or overload signs dominate and escalate because the patient-safety consequence is immediate.
confirmatoryStudies: Clinical assessment of respiratory timing and volume status
Transfusion history and product context
Blood-bank documentation with urgent clinical communication
pearls: TRALI and TACO are safety-first classifications before they are board-style vocabulary items.
Respiratory deterioration after transfusion should stay in the transfusion-hazard lane.
pitfalls: Do not collapse pulmonary transfusion hazards into a generic cardiopulmonary differential.
Do not delay escalation while waiting for perfect subtype certainty.

tr8.title: Stop: route away from transfusion-reaction triage
tr8.description: If transfusion timing, symptom pattern, and safety context do not support a true reaction pathway, leave this lane and return to the broader CP workup that best fits the case.
stopConditions: No convincing transfusion-linked temporal relationship
Symptoms are better explained by a non-transfusion process
Identity-sensitive blood-bank harm is not the central risk

title: Crossmatch Workup
summary: Stepwise blood-bank algorithm for crossmatch incompatibility, specimen identification checks, antibody-screen correlation, and safe unit-selection escalation.

cx1.title: Start
cx1.description: A red-cell unit request reaches the blood bank with a crossmatch problem, incompatibility concern, or unexpected serologic mismatch.

options.label: Begin crossmatch workup

cx2.title: Decision: active reaction versus routine pretransfusion problem
cx2.description: Decide whether this is a routine compatibility issue or an acute transfusion-safety event requiring immediate reaction workflow.
cx2.relatedTutorialQuery: DAT and Hemolytic Transfusion Reaction Studio

options.label: Active transfusion reaction or new hemolysis concern
options.rationale: Route first to immediate safety actions and reaction workup.

options.label: Routine pretransfusion incompatibility or crossmatch discrepancy
options.rationale: Proceed with compatibility-focused serologic workup.

cx3.title: Result: reaction safety path first
cx3.description: Stop transfusion if running, maintain venous access, notify the blood bank and clinical team, perform clerical check, and collect the post-transfusion workup before resuming compatibility logic.
cx3.relatedTutorialQuery: DAT and Hemolytic Transfusion Reaction Studio
pearls: Do not continue routine compatibility reasoning before reaction triage.
Clerical error and acute hemolysis must be excluded early.
pitfalls: Treating a possible hemolytic reaction as a routine crossmatch nuisance.
Delaying blood-bank notification while chasing serologies.

cx4.title: Decision: specimen identity and ABO/Rh consistency
cx4.description: Confirm patient and sample identification, prior blood-bank history, current ABO/Rh typing, and whether the discrepancy is explainable by a clerical or specimen problem.

options.label: ABO/Rh or identification inconsistency present
options.rationale: Possible wrong blood in tube, mislabeled sample, or clerical mismatch.

options.label: Identity and ABO/Rh are internally consistent
options.rationale: Move to antibody-screen and compatibility interpretation.

cx5.title: Stop: resolve specimen or clerical problem
cx5.description: Hold release, repeat identification checks, obtain a new properly labeled sample if needed, and restart pretransfusion testing only after the identity problem is closed.
stopConditions: Wrong blood in tube suspected
Sample labeling discrepancy
ABO/Rh mismatch unexplained by known transfusion history

cx6.title: Decision: antibody-screen or crossmatch pattern
cx6.description: Interpret whether the incompatibility is screen-positive, broad, or DAT or autocontrol-linked, and decide whether the pattern is more consistent with alloantibody, warm autoantibody, or a non-antibody explanation.
cx6.relatedTutorialQuery: red cell immunology and compatibility testing

options.label: Findings support alloantibody or specific compatibility issue
options.rationale: Proceed with antigen-focused compatibility strategy.

options.label: Broad incompatibility, DAT or autocontrol issue, or recent-transfusion complexity
options.rationale: Escalate into DAT-linked or autoantibody-aware workup.

cx7.title: Result: antigen-negative compatible-unit path
cx7.description: Complete panel correlation, select antigen-negative units when indicated, and issue the safest compatible product with documented serologic rationale.
cx7.relatedTutorialQuery: red cell immunology and compatibility testing
confirmatoryStudies: Antibody identification panel
Targeted antigen typing or phenotype correlation
pearls: Compatibility logic should match the specific antibody pattern.
Release should be tied to the safest serologically supportable unit choice.
pitfalls: Issuing uncategorized incompatible units before finishing focused antibody reasoning.
Confusing screen positivity with autoantibody by default.

cx8.title: Result: DAT-linked or complex compatibility escalation
cx8.description: Use DAT, autocontrol, eluate context, and recent transfusion history to determine whether the safest path is autoantibody-aware selection, specialist escalation, or risk-managed release.
cx8.relatedTutorialQuery: DAT and Hemolytic Transfusion Reaction Studio
cx8.diagnosis: Complex crossmatch discrepancy requiring escalated blood-bank interpretation
confirmatoryStudies: DAT or autocontrol correlation
Eluate or recent-transfusion contextual workup as indicated
pearls: DAT positivity alone does not prove acute incompatibility.
Recent transfusion can change how broad reactivity is interpreted.
pitfalls: Treating every broad incompatibility as an alloantibody problem.
Skipping escalation when safe unit selection remains uncertain.

title: Clinical Microbiology Specimen Quality Triage
summary: Decide whether a microbiology specimen is acceptable for culture or molecular workup, when recollection is safer, and when a limited bench path can still yield a trustworthy result.

ms1.title: Start
ms1.description: Begin with the specimen itself before focusing on the organism. In microbiology, source quality and transport conditions often determine whether any downstream interpretation is worth trusting.

options.label: Assess source quality, collection context, and transport integrity

ms2.title: Decision: is the specimen fit for microbiology interpretation?
ms2.description: Use specimen source, contamination risk, transport delay, and adequacy to decide whether the sample should be worked up, cautiously limited, or rejected.
ms2.relatedTutorialQuery: Clinical Microbiology Methods and AST Studio

options.label: Poor source control, heavy contamination risk, or transport failure makes the sample misleading
options.rationale: A compromised microbiology specimen should be stopped before the learner overinterprets colonizers or degraded material.

options.label: The sample is usable, but source quality changes how broad the workup should be
options.rationale: Some specimens are acceptable but still require bench restraint and source-aware interpretation.

ms3.title: Stop: reject or recollect before bench interpretation
ms3.description: Document the quality problem, communicate the limitation, and obtain a better specimen when the current sample cannot support safe microbiology interpretation.
ms3.relatedTutorialQuery: Clinical Microbiology Methods and AST Studio
stopConditions: Collection quality or contamination risk invalidates interpretation
Transport delay or handling failure undermines organism recovery
Source labeling or specimen adequacy is not trustworthy

ms4.title: Decision: what is the next safest microbiology bench path?
ms4.description: Once the specimen is usable, choose whether the next move is direct Gram-stain and culture triage, a narrower source-specific bench path, or a molecular-first approach.
ms4.relatedTutorialQuery: Clinical Microbiology Methods and AST Studio

options.label: Direct culture and source-aware organism workup are appropriate
options.rationale: Good-quality routine specimens can move into organism identification workflow.

options.label: The sample is usable but high-risk for misleading overgrowth or source ambiguity
options.rationale: This lane needs constrained interpretation and explicit contamination awareness.

options.label: The main value is rapid molecular detection rather than broad culture interpretation
options.rationale: Some specimens are best routed toward focused molecular or syndromic testing.

ms5.title: Result: proceed to organism identification workflow
ms5.description: Move the case into morphology, colony, MALDI-TOF, or other organism-identification logic because the specimen quality supports a standard microbiology workup.
ms5.relatedTutorialQuery: Clinical Microbiology Methods and AST Studio
ms5.diagnosis: Specimen quality supports full microbiology bench interpretation
confirmatoryStudies: Source-aware culture setup and Gram-stain review
Organism identification pathway matched to the recovered isolate
pearls: Specimen quality is part of the diagnosis because it determines whether an isolate is clinically meaningful.
A strong specimen lets the learner focus on organism reasoning instead of contamination defense.
pitfalls: Do not skip source context once growth appears convincing.
Do not separate bench choice from the actual specimen type.

ms6.title: Result: limited interpretation with contamination caution
ms6.description: Use a narrower reporting frame when the specimen is usable but vulnerable to colonizer noise, mixed flora confusion, or source-quality limitations.
ms6.relatedTutorialQuery: Clinical Microbiology Methods and AST Studio
ms6.diagnosis: Specimen quality allows only constrained microbiology interpretation
confirmatoryStudies: Correlation with source and clinical syndrome
Selective workup of dominant or clearly significant organisms
pearls: Not every usable specimen deserves a maximal workup.
Constrained interpretation is often safer than false precision.
pitfalls: Do not overcall mixed or low-quality recovery as true infection.
Do not let bench enthusiasm outrun source credibility.

ms7.title: Result: molecular-first or targeted rapid-detection path
ms7.description: Route toward rapid molecular or syndromic testing when specimen quality is adequate and the main clinical value is timely pathogen detection rather than a full conventional culture pathway.
ms7.relatedTutorialQuery: Clinical Microbiology Methods and AST Studio
ms7.diagnosis: Specimen quality supports targeted rapid microbiology testing
confirmatoryStudies: Focused molecular assay selection
Correlation with specimen source and expected pathogen class
pearls: A molecular-first path is still governed by specimen quality.
Rapid detection helps most when it answers a clinically urgent question.
pitfalls: Do not treat every specimen as equally suited for syndromic testing.
Do not ignore source-quality limitations just because a rapid platform is available.

title: AST Interpretation
summary: Interpret antimicrobial susceptibility results only after organism identity, source context, and reporting consequences are aligned, then decide when to suppress, escalate, or communicate a high-risk result.

ast1.title: Start
ast1.description: Begin AST interpretation only after the organism and source are credible enough that the susceptibility profile will affect real treatment decisions.

options.label: Frame the isolate, source, and reporting stakes before reading categories

ast2.title: Decision: is the organism-source pairing trustworthy enough for AST reporting?
ast2.description: Susceptibility categories are only meaningful when the isolate itself is clinically relevant and the specimen source supports treatment-level interpretation.
ast2.relatedTutorialQuery: Clinical Microbiology Methods and AST Studio

options.label: The isolate is probably contaminant or source relevance is weak
options.rationale: AST can mislead stewardship if the organism should not be driving therapy.

options.label: The isolate is clinically meaningful and ready for susceptibility interpretation
options.rationale: Now the learner can interpret categories in the right clinical frame.

ast3.title: Stop: do not over-report AST on a weak isolate
ast3.description: Reassess source relevance or recollect rather than pushing susceptibility data that could falsely imply a treatable pathogen.
ast3.relatedTutorialQuery: Clinical Microbiology Methods and AST Studio
stopConditions: Isolate likely reflects contamination or colonization
Specimen source does not support treatment-driving interpretation
AST reporting would create more confusion than value

ast4.title: Decision: what kind of AST reporting problem is this?
ast4.description: Separate routine interpretable results from resistant, suppressed, or escalation-worthy patterns before final reporting.
ast4.relatedTutorialQuery: Clinical Microbiology Methods and AST Studio

options.label: The profile is straightforward and internally consistent for routine reporting
options.rationale: A routine pattern can move into standard source-aware reporting.

options.label: The profile suggests resistance mechanism, suppression rule, or stewardship-sensitive escalation
options.rationale: Complex or high-risk AST patterns need a more deliberate interpretation path.

ast5.title: Result: routine AST reporting path
ast5.description: Report the susceptibility profile in a source-aware way, keeping the organism identity, likely therapy relevance, and local bench conventions aligned.
ast5.relatedTutorialQuery: Clinical Microbiology Methods and AST Studio
ast5.diagnosis: Routine AST interpretation supports direct reporting
confirmatoryStudies: AST category review matched to organism identity
Source-aware selection of reportable agents
pearls: AST categories are not free-floating facts; they belong to a specific organism and source.
Routine results still deserve reporting discipline.
pitfalls: Do not report every tested drug if the source or organism context makes some misleading.
Do not detach susceptibility categories from the isolate that generated them.

ast6.title: Decision: is this mainly a resistance-pattern problem or a reporting-governance problem?
ast6.description: Some AST cases are hard because the organism is resistant; others are hard because selective suppression or communication rules control what should be released.
ast6.relatedTutorialQuery: Clinical Microbiology Methods and AST Studio

options.label: Resistance mechanism or multidrug-resistance concern dominates
options.rationale: This needs escalation around resistant phenotype interpretation and stewardship impact.

options.label: Selective reporting, suppression, or communication rules dominate
options.rationale: This needs a reporting-governance frame more than a pure resistance frame.

ast7.title: Result: resistant phenotype escalation path
ast7.description: Escalate when the AST pattern suggests multidrug resistance, discordant phenotype concerns, or a high-stakes treatment implication that should not be treated as routine.
ast7.relatedTutorialQuery: Clinical Microbiology Methods and AST Studio
ast7.diagnosis: AST interpretation suggests resistant phenotype requiring higher-level review
confirmatoryStudies: Phenotype review in light of organism identity
Additional susceptibility or resistance-confirmation workup when indicated
Timely clinical communication for high-risk findings
pearls: High-risk AST patterns are part interpretation problem and part communication problem.
Stewardship consequence is one reason these results matter.
pitfalls: Do not bury resistant patterns inside routine release language.
Do not overcall a mechanism without respecting the organism context.

ast8.title: Result: selective reporting and stewardship path
ast8.description: Use suppression logic, selective release, and source-aware communication when the main task is deciding what the clinician should actually see and act on.
ast8.relatedTutorialQuery: Clinical Microbiology Methods and AST Studio
ast8.diagnosis: AST reporting requires stewardship-aware selective release
confirmatoryStudies: Review of suppression and selective-reporting policy
Clinical communication of the most actionable agents or restrictions
pearls: Selective AST reporting is a patient-safety and stewardship tool, not just a formatting choice.
What is withheld can matter as much as what is shown.
pitfalls: Do not release technically available AST data that undermines stewardship policy.
Do not forget that suppression rules still need clear clinical communication.

title: Organism Identification Workflow
summary: Choose the next safest organism-identification path by combining Gram stain, growth pattern, source context, and bench method selection before attaching clinical meaning or susceptibility consequences.

oid1.title: Start
oid1.description: Begin organism identification only after the specimen is good enough to support microbiology interpretation, then decide which bench signal should drive the next step.

options.label: Integrate source context with the first bench clues

oid2.title: Decision: what is the strongest early identification signal?
oid2.description: Use Gram stain, colony pattern, growth conditions, or rapid platform fit to determine the next identification branch instead of reflexively applying every method.
oid2.relatedTutorialQuery: Clinical Microbiology Methods and AST Studio

options.label: Gram stain or colony pattern already narrows the organism class
options.rationale: Morphology and growth pattern can often choose the bench path before higher-complexity testing.

options.label: The isolate needs a faster or more definitive platform-directed identification step
options.rationale: Some organisms are best clarified through MALDI-TOF or a targeted rapid method.

oid3.title: Decision: is the morphology-source pairing coherent?
oid3.description: Decide whether the observed pattern matches a plausible pathogen-source story or whether mixed growth, contamination concern, or unexpected morphology should slow the interpretation.
oid3.relatedTutorialQuery: Clinical Microbiology Methods and AST Studio

options.label: Pattern is coherent and can proceed through a standard organism workup
options.rationale: A consistent early pattern supports a conventional identification branch.

options.label: Pattern is mixed, unexpected, or contamination-prone
options.rationale: Incoherent patterns need restrained interpretation and source re-check before naming the organism.

oid4.title: Result: rapid-platform or targeted identification path
oid4.description: Move to MALDI-TOF, focused biochemical logic, or another targeted rapid method when the next identification step should maximize clarity without unnecessary bench sprawl.
oid4.relatedTutorialQuery: Clinical Microbiology Methods and AST Studio
confirmatoryStudies: Platform-directed identification matched to organism class
Source-aware correlation before final naming
pearls: The fastest method is useful only when it is the right method for the organism class.
Rapid identification still depends on bench coherence and source logic.
pitfalls: Do not choose a platform just because it is available.
Do not let a rapid result outrun source credibility.

oid5.title: Result: standard organism workup path
oid5.description: Proceed through a conventional morphology, colony, and focused identification pathway because the early bench pattern and specimen source support a stable organism story.
oid5.relatedTutorialQuery: Clinical Microbiology Methods and AST Studio
confirmatoryStudies: Focused morphology and colony review
Follow-on identification matched to the narrowed organism class
pearls: Good organism identification is a sequence of narrowing decisions, not a jump straight to a label.
Source context protects against overreading a plausible-looking isolate.
pitfalls: Do not skip the narrowing steps once the broad class is known.
Do not treat every colony as equally clinically meaningful.

oid6.title: Result: constrained organism-identification path
oid6.description: Use a limited or cautious identification frame when mixed growth, contamination concern, or source mismatch makes full organism naming unsafe or low-yield.
oid6.relatedTutorialQuery: Clinical Microbiology Methods and AST Studio
confirmatoryStudies: Selective follow-up on dominant or source-plausible isolates
Clinical or specimen-quality correlation before final release
pearls: Sometimes the right answer is a restrained workup, not maximal organism naming.
A cautious organism ID path can prevent false downstream AST confidence.
pitfalls: Do not force full identification out of mixed or low-trust growth.
Do not separate naming decisions from contamination risk.
